question: How do memos work on Stellar: types, size limit, case-sensitivity, whether one transaction can carry multiple, which transactions support them, and why do exchanges require them?

golden.answer: A Stellar memo is optional **transaction-level** metadata; a classic transaction carries at most one. Types are `MEMO_TEXT` (UTF-8, up to 28 bytes), `MEMO_ID` (uint64), `MEMO_HASH` (32 bytes), and `MEMO_RETURN` (32-byte refund-reference hash). Exchanges/anchors/custodians use it to map deposits to customers sharing one pooled G-account; copy the service's value exactly because matching/case policy is application-defined. Ordinary classic transactions may carry the memo. A fee-bump outer envelope has no second memo—the memo, if any, is inside the inner transaction. On current Protocol 27, an `InvokeHostFunction` Soroban transaction must use `MEMO_NONE`; do not assume every transaction form accepts a memo.
keyFacts: Names memo types/limits, one-per-transaction placement, and pooled-custody purpose.
Explains fee-bump inner placement and the current InvokeHostFunction MEMO_NONE restriction.
avoid: Do NOT claim multiple memos, treat a memo as an account/operation field, or assume Soroban InvokeHostFunction accepts one.
Do NOT normalize exchange memo text without the recipient's policy.
golden.notes:
GT-29 CORRECTION 2026-07-10: transaction support is explicitly asked and therefore gating. Fee-bump has no second outer memo; current InvokeHostFunction transactions require MEMO_NONE.
GT-29 blind completeness correction; base mechanics independently confirmed by primary.

corroboration.claim: Classic transactions carry at most one memo; fee-bump has no second outer memo, and current InvokeHostFunction transactions require MEMO_NONE.
